response:
## Decision: Run the full pre-registered grid (Option A) — no early stop on a single-arm pilot

**Choice.** Execute all 4 arms (Haiku-solo / Sonnet-solo / Opus-solo / Tiered) across the pre-screened 8 tasks × 3 reps as pre-registered (ISSUE-41.md:33-43, 68-69). The only sanctioned adaptivity is the pre-registered sequential-escalation clause (line 49) — add reps/tasks when a CI straddles the boundary — applied within this fixed 4-arm design. No arm is piloted alone, and no arm is skipped or deferred based on an early read.

**Rejected: pilot Opus-solo (or any single arm) first and stop early if the gate looks decidable.** The decision rule at line 51 is a function of Tiered and Sonnet-solo only (delta vs Sonnet-solo, tokens/wall-clock vs Sonnet-solo). No single-arm pilot — including the "most expensive" one — can ever produce a decidable read on that rule; at minimum Tiered+Sonnet-solo must run together. A pilot framed as "stop early if the gate is decidable" is therefore not a leaner version of the same test, it's a different, non-load-bearing measurement (Opus-solo alone speaks only to the secondary "does Tiered reach Opus-solo quality" report) dressed up as if it could settle adoption.

**The single load-bearing assumption:** *the sequential-escalation clause at line 49 ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") is scoped to reps/tasks within the fixed 4-arm design, not to skipping or reordering whole arms.* Everything about running the full grid rather than piloting rests on reading line 49 narrowly. If that's wrong — if the owner actually intended line 49's escalation license to license arm-level triage (e.g., "don't bother running Haiku-solo/Opus-solo until the Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo comparison is inconclusive") — then this decision over-spends: it runs ~250-300 invocations up front (line 71) when a leaner staged design (Tiered+Sonnet-solo first, escalate to Haiku-solo/Opus-solo only if needed) would have sufficed and cost meaningfully less.

**The failure class I'm accepting:** over-spend / lower efficiency, not invalidity. Worst case, this run spends tokens on Haiku-solo and Opus-solo data that turns out to be unnecessary because Tiered vs Sonnet-solo alone was already unambiguous — pure cost against the ~250-300-invocation estimate. I'm accepting this over the alternative failure class (Assumption Probe's Option B risk, sharpened by the Premortem Probe): an arm-order stopping rule is an undisclosed second adaptive-stopping mechanism layered on top of the one pre-registered, reopening the exact fishing risk pre-registration exists to close (line 25/27), and it risks the pre-screen's Haiku-Opus gradient check (line 42) and the quality-vs-cost frontier (line 48) being silently left incomplete while ADR 0024's "record the result either way" (line 18) reads as if the full pre-registered test ran. Between paying for data that might go unused and shipping a study whose completeness and provenance can't be defended under later audit, the former is the correct trade — pre-registration's value comes from running the committed design, not from being maximally cheap.

**Not resolved by this decision, and blocking regardless of arm order:** the owner call at line 73 item 1 (grader independence is unsolvable with 3 tiers once Opus-solo runs — accept the Sonnet-grades-Sonnet residual or defer). This call is upstream of *any* arm running, including a pilot, and must be made before Opus-solo data is generated under either option.
